title: Aliases
content:
### SQL Aliases

Aliases give a table or column a temporary name within a query. They make queries more readable and are essential for self-joins and subqueries.

---

### Column Aliases

Use the AS keyword to rename a column in the result set:

```sql
SELECT name AS employee_name,
       salary * 12 AS annual_salary
FROM employees;
```

The AS keyword is optional:

```sql
SELECT name employee_name FROM employees;
```

### Table Aliases

Table aliases shorten table names, especially useful with joins:

```sql
SELECT e.name, d.department_name
FROM employees AS e
JOIN departments AS d ON e.dept_id = d.id;
```

### Aliases with Spaces

If an alias contains spaces or special characters, enclose it in double quotes or square brackets:

```sql
SELECT name AS "Employee Name"
FROM employees;

-- SQL Server
SELECT name AS [Employee Name]
FROM employees;
```

### When Aliases are Required

- Self-joins (joining a table to itself)
- Subqueries in FROM clause (derived tables)
- When two tables have columns with the same name

| Use Case | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Self-join | FROM employees e1 JOIN employees e2 |
| Derived table | FROM (SELECT ...) AS sub |
| Ambiguous columns | SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e JOIN departments d |

> **Key Point:** Aliases are scoped to the current query only. They do not change the actual table or column names in the database.
codeExample:
SELECT p.name AS product_name,
       p.price AS unit_price,
       o.quantity
FROM products AS p
JOIN orders AS o ON p.id = o.product_id;

quiz.question: Which keyword is used to create an alias in SQL?
